other_observers: Jean-Pierre Georges, not a trained birder; I was original, but the following morning, and many subsequent days, more skilled birders than I confirmed this sighting.
specific_location: Gregory Canyon in OSMP parks, west side of town; near trailhead & parking lot (within 30 ft)
description: Rather dark gray-greenish upper body--not remarkable in shade--but contrasting with much brighter yellow underbody, throat and especially supercilium. Log notes include "unique zigzaggy black mask going from general eye area toward shoulder & partwaydown side of throat." No white (eyering or wingbars for example) anywhere visible. Generally quite plain except for mask.  Size typical of parulidae: smaller than thrush family for example.  There was no time to note more refined details.Behaviors: Skulky lurking in dense riparian undergrowth; periods of foraging, of singing (brief--a minute, then long silence of up to 5 or more minutes), of suspicious avoidance activity when others were on the trail. In this way, a thrush or a montane sparrow might have been suspected had not song & appearance been completely different.Call: simple, resounding, even brilliant "double-syllable" upslurred notes, reminding me instantly of song quality of Carolina wren (with which I am much more familiar). Song notes sounded echo-ey due to bird's position, constantly in thick low underbrush.Plumage: breeding
